plainMeaning: Here the worshipper bows to the Jina-shrines of the towns of Khambhāt, Tārāpur, Mātar and Gandhār, and venerates the two famous Pārśvanātha images named Lodhaṇ and Cintāmaṇi.
recitation: khaṁbhāyata ne tārāpure, sā° || mātara ne gandhāra | lodhaṇa cintāmaṇi varu, sā°

wordByWord.gloss: Khambhāt (Cambay), the port-town shrine; Sanskrit Stambhatīrtha / Stambhapura, a Pārśvanātha tīrtha
wordByWord.etymology: From Skt. stambha-tīrtha 'pillar-ford' → Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa khaṁbhāïta → Guj. khaṁbhāyat; the old harbour-city on the Gulf of Khambhāt.
wordByWord.notes: Named in the dhāla's tīrthamālā; tradition links it to Ācārya Abhayadeva and to Pārśvanātha.

wordByWord.etymology: Old/Middle Gujarati copulative conjunction ne, 'and', joining the listed place-names.
wordByWord.notes: Recurs as the list-connector throughout the verse.

wordByWord.gloss: at Tārāpur (the Tārāpur shrine, sacred to Śaṅkheśvara Pārśvanātha)
wordByWord.etymology: Tārā + pura 'town of Tārā'; locative -e ('at/in'), a town in central Gujarat.
wordByWord.notes: Locative case marks it as a place where one bows.

wordByWord.gloss: [the dhāla's refrain, abbreviated] — the recurring chant-word beginning sā‑, marked here by the circle ૦ rather than written out
wordByWord.etymology: Scribal abbreviation: the trailing ૦ (vartula) signals the dhāla's fixed refrain-word (dhruva/ṭeka) that the singer supplies in full.
wordByWord.notes: The verse gives only the cue sā°; the full refrain-word is established at the dhāla's opening, not within this verse (see notes).

wordByWord.etymology: Daṇḍa pair marking a caesura within the line, part of the book's printed orthography.

wordByWord.gloss: Mātar (the Mātar shrine of Sumatinātha / Pārśvanātha, in Kheḍā district)
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. place-name mātar; a pilgrimage town near Nadiad in central Gujarat.
wordByWord.notes: Renowned as a 'Saccā Dev' tīrtha in Gujarat tradition.

wordByWord.gloss: Gandhār (the Gandhār shrine near Bharuch, sacred to Amīzarā Pārśvanātha / Mahāvīra)
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. gandhāra; an old port-village on the Narmadā estuary in Bharuch district.
wordByWord.notes: An ancient coastal tīrtha of the Gujarat belt.

wordByWord.etymology: Daṇḍa closing the hemistich, part of the printed text.

wordByWord.gloss: Lodhaṇ (Lodhaṇ Pārśvanātha — a celebrated, ancient black-stone Pārśva image)
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. proper name lodhaṇ, the epithet of a particular Pārśvanātha icon counted among the renowned Pārśva images.
wordByWord.notes: Here a named image (pratimā), shifting the list from towns to famous icons.

wordByWord.gloss: Cintāmaṇi (Cintāmaṇi Pārśvanātha — the 'wish-granting jewel' Pārśva image, esp. of Khambhāt)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. cintā 'thought, worry' + maṇi 'jewel' = the gem that fulfils every desire; an epithet of a Pārśvanātha icon.
wordByWord.notes: A wish-fulfilling-gem epithet applied to a Pārśva image.

wordByWord.gloss: I venerate / I choose (as the most excellent)
wordByWord.etymology: From Skt. √vṛ (vṛṇoti/vṛṇute) 'to choose, select as a boon, adore'; 1st-person reverential 'I choose/venerate'; cf. vara 'excellent, chosen'.
wordByWord.notes: Reads as the worshipper's first-person act of choosing-in-reverence; can also be felt adjectivally ('the excellent') qualifying the images.

wordByWord.etymology: Repeated refrain-cue closing the second line, as at the first.
wordByWord.notes: Marks the sung return to the dhāla's fixed refrain.

literalTranslation: Khambhāt and at Tārāpur — [refrain] — Mātar and Gandhār; Lodhaṇ [and] Cintāmaṇi I venerate — [refrain].
idiomaticTranslation: I bow at Khambhāt and Tārāpur — and at Mātar and Gandhār too; and to Lodhaṇ Pārśva and Cintāmaṇi Pārśva, most excellent, I give my reverence.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the previous line's sweep through Amīzarā, Navapallava, Navakhaṇḍa-Thārād, Tāraṅgā and Burhānpur, this couplet draws the garland inward to the Pārśvanātha heartland of central and coastal Gujarat. Four living pilgrim-towns are named in a single breath — Khambhāt, Tārāpur, Mātar, Gandhār — each a working shrine on the medieval trade-and-pilgrimage road of the Gulf of Khambhāt, and each strongly tied to the twenty-third Jina, Pārśvanātha. The line then performs a small but deliberate turn: from places to icons. Lodhaṇ and Cintāmaṇi are not towns but the proper names of two renowned Pārśva images, so the worshipper's bow narrows from temple-towns (caitya) to the very pratimās enshrined in them — exactly the image-veneration that gives the caitya-vandana its name. 'Cintāmaṇi', the wish-granting jewel, is the most charged word here: by naming an image so, the poet quietly claims that gazing on the Jina fulfils every longing — while the verb varu ('I choose, I venerate') makes the act personal and present-tense, the reciter selecting these holy forms here and now. The verse then hands on to the next line, which carries the gaze westward to Devapattan, Devagiri, Navānagar and the seaports — Gujarat's coast and Saurashtra. So this couplet is the central-Gujarat 'station' of the tīrthamālā, dense with Pārśvanātha precisely because that Jina dominated the region's living devotion. The doubled sā° refrain keeps the whole as song, each name dropped onto the same returning melodic peg.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse enacts the core logic of caitya-vandana: reverence reaches the formless, liberated Jina through located form — named towns and named images. Naming an icon 'Cintāmaṇi' encodes a teaching about bhakti: the Jina grants no worldly boons by intervention (a siddha is utterly detached), yet sincere darśana of his image purifies the seer's own karma, so the 'wish-fulfilling' power is the devotee's transformed mind, not the stone. The first-person varu (√vṛ, 'to choose') frames pilgrimage as an act of will — samyaktva expressed as deliberate selection of the right objects of reverence — consonant with the hymn's aim of strengthening right faith through universe-wide bowing.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this line each dawn, a worshipper who may never travel to Khambhāt or Gandhār still makes the pilgrimage in spirit, mentally bowing to each shrine and image. Practically, let varu register as a present choice: name each place, picture the Pārśva image, and re-choose reverence today — turning a list of towns into a brief, attentive interior darśana rather than a recited inventory.

crossReferences.text: Stambhatīrtha (Khambhāt) as an ancient Pārśvanātha tīrtha founded in Abhayadeva's circle

sources.title: Stambhapura / Stambhatīrtha (Khambhāt) — definition and history
sources.consultedFor: Identifying ખંભાયત as Stambhatīrtha/Khambhāt, a medieval Pārśvanātha tīrtha associated with Abhayadeva and the Vividhatīrthakalpa.

sources.title: Cintāmaṇi — definition (the wish-granting jewel)
sources.consultedFor: Sense of ચિંતામણિ as the desire-fulfilling gem, grounding the epithet of the Cintāmaṇi Pārśvanātha image.

sources.title: Vṛ (√vṛ) — Sanskrit verbal root 'to choose, select as a boon, adore'
sources.consultedFor: Etymology and reverential sense of વરુ (varu) as 1st-person 'I choose/venerate', and the related noun vara 'excellent/chosen'.

notes: Place identifications are secure: ખંભાયત = Khambhāt/Stambhatīrtha, તારાપુરે = Tārāpur, માતર = Mātar, ગંધાર = Gandhār — all central/coastal-Gujarat Pārśvanātha tīrthas. લોઢણ and ચિંતામણિ are read as proper names of two renowned Pārśvanātha images (the line shifting from towns to icons); this image-reading, rather than two further towns, is the most coherent but is interpretive. વરુ is taken as a 1st-person reverential of √vṛ ('I choose/venerate'); an adjectival reading (vara, 'the excellent') qualifying the images is also possible and the rendering reflects both. The cue 'સા૦' is the dhāla's refrain abbreviated by the circle (૦); its full expansion is fixed at the dhāla's opening (the dhruva/ṭeka) and cannot be recovered from this verse alone, so it is left as a refrain-cue. Khambhāt also appears among the dhāla-8 places noted in the preface, so a degree of repetition across the tīrthamālā is expected. Spelling kept verbatim per the Anchalgaccha (Vidhipaksha) edition.
